speaker: interviewer
text: --till the war ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. [? Albers ?] was sent back to-- to Germany with the kid, and I didn't want to go with them. I stayed in the apartment till that Russian came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was January, I think, 1945. As a matter of fact, I was freed a day before or a day after Mayer's birthday, and I thought this was symbolic. And when-- when the Russians came, they came with huge tanks and they-- they were behaving really beastly. And I did not want to stay in that apartment. I changed this apartment with another woman because she had a big family and I got the little kitchen and a room. And I moved everything there, and all the rest was staying in that apartment. And when the war-- war was ending, people ran out, and I saw for first time those huge tanks, and the soldiers sitting on them. And they were shouting, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], means for Stalin and for the family. And Polish girls were jumping on their laps and-- and singing and sitting. And I considered myself sophisticated, and I said, you see, there is a mass psychology till I turned around. I shouted with all the people, for freedom! For the family! For-- for Russia! That I didn't even know that I was doing that. And then after that, I went back to-- to my-- this apartment that I changed with this Polish lady and I was waiting till my brother will come, till Mayer will come. Mayer-- Mayer knew my address in Czechoslovakia before I knew that he survived. And he was-- and-- wait a second--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But he was in-- yeah, but he was at Theresienstadt. And the people were going back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew your address in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Piotrkow, this other-- not the latest, Piotrkow. And-- so what happened is that I said that I'm going to wait in Krakow. Maybe my father is going to come. Maybe my brother is going to come. And I hope for my mother and my little sister, but I knew that this was just impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was happening that I am waiting and so often I went back to Piotrkow to find out if somebody came back. I came back. I found my cousin, and my cousin told me-- she-- she took me down-- downtown and said don't wait for your father, because your father died in [INAUDIBLE]. This was a subcamp of Auschwitz, and I saw him dying.

speaker: interviewee
text: He died of a typhoid, and he was always talking about you and [? Benyik, ?] that you will survive and that his children will survive. And he was healthy, but he died of a typhoid epidemic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I will say that the worst time for me was after the war, when you go back to your city and you hear this one died, and this didn't come back, this one didn't come back and this one didn't come back. What-- it is devastating. You know? And what happened is one day, my brother came. And when my brother came, I had my address. He came to see me in the house in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, [? Benyik, ?] now we have to-- I already knew that my father isn't coming-- now we have to wait for Mayer, because I was told that they have seen him in Theresienstadt not so long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you will go occasionally to-- to Piotrkow to see and wait for Mayer. And I am going to keep watch the house, because you cannot just leave the house. Somebody could break in and take your apartment. Then you will be living on the street. So he went, and one day he came to Piotrkow and Mayer came back from Theresienstadt. And this was summer, I think, and my-- my brother went where they gave him a place on an attic, and he said, Mayer, let's go home to [? Ruska. ?] That's how they came home. And Mayer will tell you, I still didn't understand how, because he was nice fed up. He looked good. But he said-- he will tell it to you later that when I was serving, you know, family-style soup and potatoes, and I wanted to make everything nice. And he looked at this and said, this is a soup, those are the potatoes for everybody? I can eat that by myself, just like that. But he never told me that, never. OK? When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: September '45, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've been married 50 years, 51 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we had our 50th wedding-- wedding anniversary last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go from Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Krakow, Mayer started to work. Mayer was started to work with the-- with the youth of [INAUDIBLE]. I went and wanted to teach. There was a little kibbutz there, and I wanted to teach them geography, but they did not want me to teach in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow. You know, they were coming from Russia, and they didn't want to learn Polish. And as a matter of fact, they didn't want to listen to me speaking everything in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Then where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, from there, I went to-- no-- what was the camp? Not Ludwigsdorf. The camp was called-- to a camp in Germany, a displaced persons camp. No, wait a second. I started to-- I am correcting myself. I started to work in Krakow right away with children. The children were sent to-- to [INAUDIBLE]. I went to the children to [INAUDIBLE], And this was Ludwigsdorf. The place was called Ludwigsdorf. And from there, the children were sent on aliyah back, and we went to organize the orphanage. I forgot what the name was. I will remember later. But it was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start the orphanage, or it was ongoing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The orphanage was going with one Lithuanian girl who was-- who was doing it. And it still was in Krakow. The two of us were doing it, and I don't remember where she went. I went with the children to Ludwigsdorf, was it, Mayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I will look it up. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened, we wanted to go on aliyah, because we were Zionists. Mayer-- Mayer got sick with tuberculosis. And this was in the time of the Ma'abarot in Israel, and they didn't want to take us. They said, you stay here in Germany till Mayer gets well, and-- and then they didn't want to take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was you and Mayer or did you have any children by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I had one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his or her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Aviram. And it was in a DP camp in-- close to Stuttgart. I don't remember the name. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: When all those refugees were going back and forth, he ran away with other boys, and they pushed him in-- into the sewer. It was a open sewer where the butchers were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] slaughtering kosher meat. It was not covered up or nothing. And I remember later--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- he didn't live to be three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here you had saved all these children, but couldn't save your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't. And sometimes, you see, there is the irony of life. I couldn't save my own child. And the child was smart and sweet and Mayer came back from the sanatorium and we were together a very short time. For a short time we were a happy family, and then the kid got pushed in-- into the sewer. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were a kid came running back, and, see, I at the time was teaching kindergarten in that refugee camp because I wanted to have my son with me. So he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Somebody pushed Avi in the water. That's how I found. And they couldn't get him out. And from-- from Munich came some American group that they drained the-- the sewer and then finally they got the kid out. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to-- to the funeral. He's buried in Munich. And you never know. We needed a minyan to the cemetery, and men I have never known, you know, came on the train, and one man came over to me and said, you don't remember me, but you were a teacher to my stepchildren, and he already broke up with this wife in the meantime, but he came to-- to be in-- in the minyan.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: '51, I think, or '52. '51. Yeah. I came to the United States in '51, and we came to South Bend, Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Jewish Federation was-- was handing out permits or whatever they were calling, that they will provide the refugees with housing, with work, and that they will not be a burden to the American society. And for us, once we didn't go to Israel, South Bend, Indiana was just as good or just as bad as any other place. But then they-- they took us in a place and they gave us, everyone, a piece of clothes a nice, gorgeous wine-red robe. I was slim. A chenille robe. And I looked at the hanger, and it said Francis Shop, South Bend, Indiana. And I said, Mayer, it's beshert we're going to South Bend, Indiana, because all the Jews wanted to go to New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get the robe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had it here, and I made it-- Harvey had a costume as King [INAUDIBLE] or whatever for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in South Bend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had how many more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two more children. No, three. My daughter, see, and-- and my daughter Regina. She's a psychiatric social worker. My son, Harvey, he's an anesthesiologist, and he's writing papers. He's a doctor. And my other one is a business representative, Howard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that we're coming close to ending, is there anything you left out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I left out one thing about my first job. When I send my brother, to-- he was going to work someplace, I found for myself following [? Tosha ?] [? Altman's ?] advice, I found myself a job from the Warsaw newspaper and says, a lady to-- to cook for three men and will teach her to do some other jobs. I went to this-- to her house. What was happening, her husband in a city which was called Radzymin, not far from Warsaw, had a boot shop. And she said, you will go there and you're going to cook for all the men, three men, and my daughter comes with you. She stays there. And then she said, we will teach you how to do-- after you done, every day with cleaning up the kitchen, with cooking, we will teach you how to sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what? My first job, when I finally got it under control, he was cutting-- making boots. And he-- there-- there is a form, a pattern, and he showed me how to put the pattern on the boots. And then I wasn't allowed to do the leather, only the lining. And-- and he showed me, you will put the glue on it, and you will knock it with the thing. And then-- and then the main guy will assemble those boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I saw what I was knocking with a hammer and gluing-- those were Torah scrolls that he stole from the Radzymin shul. He said, I'm going to keep some of those because the Jews are going to pay me big money when they come back. But in the meantime, he said, see, this is good leather. And you know, every-- I had to be so calm. I have to be so composed. I have to laugh at those Jewish jokes and it was like my heart, you know, was bleeding every time I knocked the hammer on this Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the minute or so then we have left, do you have any final thoughts? Or-- Why do you think you survived? Or what message would you leave your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I think that I survived because I tried to take command of the-- of the situation. And the worst thing I was saying I am thinking was the shoes-- how do you say it? I'm thinking with the shoes on or whatever. I forgot how is it said. I always were-- were evaluating. Then I had a good sense of humor. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were an actress, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was an actress, and this act was life was the price and you live only once. And I also want you to know that I knew one thing. I will never, ever do something which is immoral or wrong. Because if there is a other world like we were imagining, and if my parents can look up on me, I don't want them to be ashamed. I want them to know-- and this was very important-- that you know your [? Ruska ?] worked, did a good job, and did nothing that you have to be ashamed of or that I have to be ashamed of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to my grandchildren, my grandchildren are brought up Jewish. They belong to a temple and are active in-- in the youth group. And my granddaughter said to Mayer and me, you are our role model. Now what can you ask better? And on a beautiful note like that, I'll thank you on behalf of myself and Steven Spielberg and Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Thank you for sharing your story with us and Happy Hanukkah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same to you. To my left is my father Herman Guterman And this picture was made in Germany. I don't remember how many years ago. And to the right is my mother, Bella Guterman She was killed when she was 40 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- I went back to the city hall in Piotrkow, and every Jew had a-- a passport. I bribed the-- the keeper in the place, gave him a bottle of vodka, and he let me go down and look for the pictures. This is my brother Benjamin, who survived the war as a Catholic. And he died a few years ago. OK. This is myself in the-- in the ghetto in 1940. And notice that I have a-- a fur collar, because Jews couldn't have fur collars. This is [? Gerhard ?] Vurrer, the righteous Gentile who was a German soldier, and he helped me and my brother. This is my passport as a Catholic that I bought it from a-- from a Polish student, and Mayer has to fix it up, put my picture on it and finish the stamp with the size of a button on the picture. And I couldn't learn to falsify the signature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? OK. This is my false [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] birth certificate that my cousin gave it to me and the lady, [INAUDIBLE] was born in 1913, but fix it up to be 1918. That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- to my left this is me and to-- to the right my brother outside the Warsaw ghetto on Palm Sunday as Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942, probably, or 3. One of those. This is me with a child, a German child. This is Klaus [? Albers. ?] I was the nanny for him during the war. And this is Mayer and myself. And the picture is on the Starnberger See. And I don't remember when it was made. This is our son Avi, Aviram, and he died in an accident. He was pushed into a sewer. To my left, this is Harvey, our old-- our son. And he's a doctor. And Howard is the youngest, and he's a business manager someplace. To the left is Sarah, and she's reading from the Torah on her bat mitzvah. The boy in front is her brother, Michael. Next to-- in the back is my daughter Regina. She's a social worker and her husband, Steve, he's a professor of mathematics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochester, New York. This is my book And this is my card on the-- on the cover. And it was published by the Jewish Publication Society. And it won a few awards. This is my husband, Mayer, and we have been sweethearts. We grew up together, and we aging together. And we are happy together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my wife said everything there is to say about our marriage and the history of it and the future.
